7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

There is a bakery near my office that I walked past for probably three months before I ever went inside. The signage looked good from the street, the reviews online were strong, and every time I passed it there were people in there buying things. One afternoon I finally had time to stop. I pushed open the door and immediately noticed the layout was confusing. The queue system was unclear. I stood there for about ninety seconds, not knowing where to go. A staff member walked past without acknowledging me, and I left without buying anything.
I have never been back. Not because the product was bad. I never got far enough to find out. The experience of being inside the place drove me out before I reached the point where the product quality even became relevant.
Websites work exactly like this. More often than most business owners realize.
You can rank on page one of Google. You can have genuinely good content and a genuinely good product. And you can still lose a significant portion of the people who click on you because the experience of being on your site drives them away before they ever reach the part where they might buy something. The traffic was earned. The click happened. And then the site itself undid the work.
When Google Started Caring About What Happens After the Click
For most of SEO’s history, the job was essentially to get people to your site. The ranking was the goal. What happened after someone landed was treated as a separate conversation, something for the UX team or the web designer, not the SEO team.
Google’s Core Web Vitals update changed that in a meaningful way. For the first time, Google formally incorporated page experience signals into its ranking algorithm. Loading speed. Visual stability as a page loads. How quickly a page becomes interactive. These are not proxy metrics for user experience. They are direct measurements of it, and they now influence where your pages rank in search results.
The implication is significant. Two websites with comparable content quality, comparable backlink profiles, and comparable technical SEO can rank differently because one loads faster and provides a smoother experience than the other. User experience is no longer a downstream consideration that happens after you have finished doing SEO. It is part of SEO now.
This is not a technicality. It reflects something real about how Google thinks about its role. Google’s product is search results that satisfy people. A result that ranks well but delivers a bad experience is a failed outcome for Google as much as for the user. Incorporating experience signals into rankings is Google aligning its algorithm more closely with what actually produces satisfied searchers.
Page Speed Is Doing More Work Than Most People Appreciate
Speed affects two things simultaneously, and most conversations about it only address one of them.
The obvious one is rankings. Slow pages rank lower than fast pages, all else being equal, because Google has made page speed a ranking signal. That is well understood at this point.
The less discussed impact is what happens to conversion rates. Research across multiple industries consistently shows that conversion rates drop meaningfully as page load time increases beyond two to three seconds on mobile. Not slightly. Meaningfully. A page that takes five seconds to load on a mobile phone can have conversion rates less than half of what the same page achieves when it loads in under two seconds.
Think about what that means in practice. You invest in SEO, your rankings improve, and traffic increases. But if your pages are slow, a substantial portion of that traffic is leaving before converting, and you are attributing the poor conversion rate to the wrong cause. You keep producing content, keep building links, keep improving rankings, and keep wondering why the business results are not following the traffic numbers upward.
Speed is not a technical nicety. It is a revenue lever that operates independently of everything else you are doing in SEO, and it is one that tends to produce results relatively quickly once addressed properly.
UX and the Signals Google Cannot Ignore
Other than speed, the user experience factors into SEO via behavioral signals. While Google doesn’t officially disclose which behavioral data Google takes into consideration when ranking websites, there’s enough indirect proof to convince experienced SEO specialists that such signals are legitimate.
Dwell time is one. When someone clicks on your listing, spends eleven seconds on that page, then returns to the list of search results and clicks on another company’s listing, you have just sent a message that your page does not adequately meet the needs of that particular query. This will happen repeatedly enough for Google to notice. Pages that consistently fail to satisfy searchers tend to lose rankings over time, regardless of how strong their technical SEO and backlinks are.
Navigation structure matters here in ways that go beyond user convenience. A site that is easy to navigate keeps visitors moving through it, visiting more pages, spending more time, finding what they came for, and occasionally finding things they did not know they were looking for. That behavior pattern sends positive signals. A site that is confusing to navigate does the opposite, producing short sessions, high bounce rates, and the kind of behavioral data that quietly erodes rankings over time.
Mobile experience is non-negotiable in Singapore’s market. Mobile devices conduct the bulk of searches within most categories, and Google has begun indexing the mobile version of web pages before anything else. Websites that work well on the computer but are clunky and difficult to navigate on a cell phone are optimizing their content for only a small portion of their audience. That gap shows up in rankings, and it shows up in conversion rates simultaneously.
The Travelodge Campaign as a Concrete Example
Travelodge Hotels came to MediaOne SEO service with an international site architecture that needed significant structural work across Singapore, Malaysia, Australia, Hong Kong, Japan, and South Korea. The campaign involved a comprehensive overhaul of how the site was structured and how it presented itself across multiple markets simultaneously.
The result was a forty percent increase in website traffic and a ranking jump of nearly five thousand positions across their target keyword set. That is not a content result. That is what happens when the structural and experiential foundations of a large site are brought up to the standard that Google and users both require.
Site architecture improvements affect both how Google crawls and understands the site and how users navigate through it once they arrive. The two things are not separate interventions. They are the same intervention producing two different categories of benefit, one in rankings and one in user behavior, both of which feed back into marketing performance.
Why These Three Things Have to Be Managed Together
This is because the decisions being made during the implementation of these individual projects impact the other projects to cause compounding issues where there is a lack of coordination between them.
If a developer optimizes the pages to increase loading speed, they might end up making some alterations that may negatively impact the website’s ranking without the knowledge of the SEO department. If a designer is working on enhancing the UX of an important web page, then they will end up restructuring URLs or deleting some ranking content. An SEO team adding schema markup or restructuring internal links might affect page load behavior in ways that need a developer’s attention.
When these three functions operate in separate silos, you get a website that is perpetually solving problems created by decisions made elsewhere in the system. When they operate as a coordinated whole, each improvement reinforces the others. Speed improvements support better UX. Better UX supports stronger behavioral signals. Stronger behavioral signals support rankings. Better rankings bring more traffic to a site that is now faster and easier to use, which improves conversion rates.
The feedback loop runs in the positive direction instead of the negative one.
What This Means for How You Evaluate SEO Work
If your current SEO campaign is reporting on keyword rankings and organic traffic but not on page speed scores, Core Web Vitals metrics, mobile usability, bounce rates, and session duration, you are getting an incomplete picture of what is driving or limiting your results.
Rankings going up while conversion rates stay flat is a sign that the experience layer is not keeping pace with the visibility layer. Traffic increasing while revenue does not follow is often a UX problem wearing an SEO costume.
The approach MediaOne SEO takes integrates all three layers into a single campaign view. Digimetrics monitors behavioral data alongside ranking data, which means the team can see when a speed or experience issue is affecting the performance of pages that are already ranking well, rather than discovering it months later in a conversion report. Technical, content, and user experience are managed as parts of the same system because that is what they actually are. The bakery near my office had a product worth buying. The experience stopped people from ever finding that out. Getting both things right at the same time is the only version that produces the result you are actually trying to achieve.
